items.evidence_text:
Advisor: When you say “gross,” you mean before taxes and Medicare and all that—does that sound like what you’re thinking?
Client: Uh—honestly I was thinking what hits my account. Net, I guess.
Advisor: Totally normal. For planning we usually anchor on gross. If you look at the full-year gross, is it closer to $84,000 a year?
Client: Yes—gross is about $84,000 for the year.
Advisor: Perfect, I’m writing that down. And what makes up that $84,000—do you have a few different streams coming in monthly?

Client: I’d say maybe two thousand a month.
Advisor: Okay—when you say two thousand, is that including everything like utilities, groceries, insurance, and property costs? Or is that just the flexible stuff?
Client: That’s kind of everything, but I’m rounding. The more exact number is about $1,850 a month.
Advisor: Perfect—$1,850 per month. And do you have any monthly debt payments—mortgage, car loan, credit cards you’re carrying month to month?
Client: No mortgage. No loans. Credit card gets paid off.

Advisor: When you say “gross,” you mean before taxes and Medicare and all that—does that sound like what you’re thinking?
Client: Uh—honestly I was thinking what hits my account. Net, I guess.
Advisor: Totally normal. For planning we usually anchor on gross. If you look at the full-year gross, is it closer to $84,000 a year?
Client: Yes—gross is about $84,000 for the year.
Advisor: Perfect, I’m writing that down. And what makes up that $84,000—do you have a few different streams coming in monthly?

Advisor: Good. Let’s shift to assets—just to get the full picture. About how much do you have in investable assets total, across accounts you can invest—brokerage, retirement, cash, that kind of thing?
Client: Uh… three hundred thousand-ish.
Advisor: Three hundred-ish—okay. If we tighten it up, is it about $315,300 in investable assets?
Client: Yes, that sounds right—about $315,300.
Advisor: Thanks. Now I’m going to ask about the buckets inside that, and you can stop me if I’m mixing terms. Do you have a taxable investment account—like a regular brokerage—around $219,650?

Client: Yeah, there’s a small private markets thing—about $4,200.
Advisor: Okay. And then separate from investable assets, your home—do you have an estimate of the primary residence value?
Client: Yes, it’s about $68,600. It’s a small place.
Advisor: And no loan outstanding against it—no mortgage balance at all?
Client: Correct, no loan. Paid off.

Advisor: First, just to confirm household status—are you currently widowed?
Client: Yes, I’m widowed.
Advisor: Thank you. And—sorry if this sounds like a dumb question—are you in the U.S., working with a U.S. registered investment advisor setup?
Client: Yes, I’m in the U.S., and yeah, it’s the RIA thing.
Advisor: Got it—US RIA. I’ll note that.

Client: Yes, I’m in the U.S., and yeah, it’s the RIA thing.
Advisor: Got it—US RIA. I’ll note that.
Advisor: Now, risk comfort. Earlier you sounded pretty cautious—when markets swing, you’d rather protect principal than chase returns. Would you call yourself conservative, moderate, aggressive—what fits?
Client: Conservative. I don’t like big drops.
Advisor: Makes sense.

Client: A little, yeah. Not terrible though.
Advisor: Okay. Back to it—taxes. Do you have a sense of what bracket you’re landing in these days? Like 22, 24, 32, 35…
Client: Uh, I think it’s 35%.
Advisor: When you say 35%, you mean your tax bracket—like the marginal bracket—not that you pay 35% of everything, right?
Client: Wait—yeah, I mean… I thought it was basically what I pay.

Advisor: Alright, David—going back to what you said earlier about wanting things simple and steady, I want to confirm a couple household basics before we talk income and taxes.
Client: Okay, sure.
Advisor: First, just to confirm household status—are you currently widowed?
Client: Yes, I’m widowed.
Advisor: Thank you. And—sorry if this sounds like a dumb question—are you in the U.S., working with a U.S. registered investment advisor setup?

Advisor: I hear you. We’ll keep this pretty straightforward. Tell me a bit about you—what does a “normal” month look like for you now that you’re retired?
Client: Pretty quiet. I’m home a lot, errands, a little travel now and then, nothing crazy.
Advisor: Got it. And just to confirm the household basics—this is just you in the household, one adult, right?
Client: Yes, just me. One adult.
Advisor: Thanks. And David, I’m going to label you as Client 1 in my notes—does that work?

Advisor: Got it. And just to confirm the household basics—this is just you in the household, one adult, right?
Client: Yes, just me. One adult.
Advisor: Thanks. And David, I’m going to label you as Client 1 in my notes—does that work?
Client: Yeah, that’s fine. Client 1.
Advisor: Okay. Let’s start with income—when you think of your gross income for the year, before taxes, what’s the ballpark?

Advisor: Great. Going back to what you said earlier about keeping things quiet and predictable—let’s talk spending. What do your monthly expenses come out to, all-in, roughly?
Client: I’d say maybe two thousand a month.
Advisor: Okay—when you say two thousand, is that including everything like utilities, groceries, insurance, and property costs? Or is that just the flexible stuff?
Client: That’s kind of everything, but I’m rounding. The more exact number is about $1,850 a month.
Advisor: Perfect—$1,850 per month. And do you have any monthly debt payments—mortgage, car loan, credit cards you’re carrying month to month?

Advisor: Hi David—good to meet you. I’m Alex. Before we get into numbers, how are you holding up today?
Client: Oh, you know… doing okay. Some days are a little heavier, but I’m alright.
Advisor: I hear you. We’ll keep this pretty straightforward. Tell me a bit about you—what does a “normal” month look like for you now that you’re retired?
Client: Pretty quiet. I’m home a lot, errands, a little travel now and then, nothing crazy.
Advisor: Got it. And just to confirm the household basics—this is just you in the household, one adult, right?

Client: Right, I pay it off. No balance sitting there.
Advisor: Great. Now, I want to make sure I have your date of birth correct for planning—what’s your birthday, David?
Client: August 5th, 1948.
Advisor: August 5th, 1948—thank you. And just so I don’t mix terms: earlier we said your disability policy runs through August 5th, 2043. Different date, but same month and day—right?
Client: Yeah—same month and day. That’s right.

Advisor: Got it. And just to confirm the household basics—this is just you in the household, one adult, right?
Client: Yes, just me. One adult.
Advisor: Thanks. And David, I’m going to label you as Client 1 in my notes—does that work?
Client: Yeah, that’s fine. Client 1.
Advisor: Okay. Let’s start with income—when you think of your gross income for the year, before taxes, what’s the ballpark?
